(Urdu: [[ʈiːpuː sʊlt̪aːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu "Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu"), Kannada: [[ʈipːu sult̪aːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"), *Sultan Fateh Ali Sahab Tipu*; 1 December 1751 – 4 May 1799), commonly referred to as **Sher-e-Mysore** (Tiger of Mysore),[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) was the [Sultan of Mysore](/wiki/List_of_Indian_monarchs "List of Indian monarchs") from 1782 until his death in 1799.[[7]](#cite_note-8) He was a pioneer of [rocket artillery](/wiki/Rocket_artillery "Rocket artillery").[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalrymple2019243-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) He expanded the iron-cased [Mysorean rockets](/wiki/Mysorean_rockets "Mysorean rockets") and commissioned the military manual *[Fathul Mujahidin](/wiki/Fathul_Mujahidin "Fathul Mujahidin")*. The [economy](/wiki/Economy "Economy") of Mysore reached a zenith during his reign. He deployed rockets against advances of British forces and their allies during the [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars"), including the [Battle of Pollilur](/wiki/Battle_of_Pollilur_(1780) "Battle of Pollilur (1780)") and [Siege of Srirangapatna](/wiki/Siege_of_Srirangapatna_(1799) "Siege of Srirangapatna (1799)").[[11]](#cite_note-Narasimha-12)

**Tipu Sultan** (Urdu: [[ʈiːpuː sʊlt̪aːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu "Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu"), Kannada: [[ʈipːu sult̪aːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"), *Sultan Fateh Ali Sahab Tipu*; 1 December 1751 – 4 May 1799), commonly referred to as **Sher-e-Mysore** (Tiger of Mysore),[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) was the [Sultan of Mysore](/wiki/List_of_Indian_monarchs "List of Indian monarchs") from 1782 until his death in 1799.[[7]](#cite_note-8) He was a pioneer of [rocket artillery](/wiki/Rocket_artillery "Rocket artillery").[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalrymple2019243-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) He expanded the iron-cased [Mysorean rockets](/wiki/Mysorean_rockets "Mysorean rockets") and commissioned the military manual *[Fathul Mujahidin](/wiki/Fathul_Mujahidin "Fathul Mujahidin")*. The [economy](/wiki/Economy "Economy") of Mysore reached a zenith during his reign. He deployed rockets against advances of British forces and their allies during the [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars"), including the [Battle of Pollilur](/wiki/Battle_of_Pollilur_(1780) "Battle of Pollilur (1780)") and [Siege of Srirangapatna](/wiki/Siege_of_Srirangapatna_(1799) "Siege of Srirangapatna (1799)").[[11]](#cite_note-Narasimha-12)

Tipu Sultan and his father [Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali") used their French-trained army in alliance with the French in their struggle with the British,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201177-13) and in Mysore's struggles with other surrounding powers: against the [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"), [Sira](/wiki/Sira,_India "Sira, India"), and rulers of [Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_(Northern_Kerala) "Malabar (Northern Kerala)"), [Kodagu](/wiki/Kodagu_district "Kodagu district"), [Bednore](/wiki/Keladi_Nayaka_Kingdom "Keladi Nayaka Kingdom"), [Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_region "Carnatic region"), and [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore"). Tipu became the ruler of Mysore upon his father's death from cancer in 1782 during the [Second Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Mysore_War "Second Anglo-Mysore War"). He negotiated with the British in 1784 with the [Treaty of Mangalore](/wiki/Treaty_of_Mangalore "Treaty of Mangalore") which ended the war in [status quo ante bellum](/wiki/Status_quo_ante_bellum "Status quo ante bellum").

Tipu's conflicts with his neighbours included the [Maratha–Mysore War](/wiki/Maratha%E2%80%93Mysore_War "Maratha–Mysore War"), which ended with the signing of the [Treaty of Gajendragad](/wiki/Gajendragarh#Treaty_of_Gajendragad "Gajendragarh").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005105–107-14)

Tipu remained an enemy of the British [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"). He initiated an [attack](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Nedumkotta "Battle of the Nedumkotta") on British-allied Travancore in 1789. In the [Third Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Mysore_War "Third Anglo-Mysore War"), he was forced into the [Treaty of Seringapatam](/wiki/Treaty_of_Seringapatam "Treaty of Seringapatam"), losing a number of previously conquered territories, including Malabar and [Mangalore](/wiki/Mangalore "Mangalore"). In the [Fourth Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Fourth_Anglo-Mysore_War "Fourth Anglo-Mysore War"), a combined force of British East India Company troops supported by the [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Confederacy "Maratha Confederacy") and the [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad") defeated Tipu. He was killed on 4 May 1799 while [defending his stronghold of Seringapatam](/wiki/Siege_of_Seringapatam_(1799) "Siege of Seringapatam (1799)").

Tipu also introduced administrative innovations during his rule, including a new coinage system and calendar,[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005399-15) and a new land revenue system, which initiated the growth of the [Mysore silk](/wiki/Mysore_silk "Mysore silk") industry.[[15]](#cite_note-Global_Silk_Industry-16) He is known for his patronage to [Channapatna toys](/wiki/Channapatna_toys "Channapatna toys").[[16]](#cite_note-Handmade-17)

## Early years

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Birth_place_of_Tipu_Sultan_%2C_01.jpg/250px-Birth_place_of_Tipu_Sultan_%2C_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birth_place_of_Tipu_Sultan_,_01.jpg)

Birth place of Tipu Sultan

### Childhood

Tipu Sultan was born in [Devanahalli](/wiki/Devanahalli "Devanahalli"), in present-day [Bangalore Rural district](/wiki/Bangalore_Rural_district "Bangalore Rural district"), about 33 km (21 mi) north of [Bangalore](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore") on 1 December 1751.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan20056-18)[[18]](#cite_note-N18_20210719-19) He was named "Tipu Sultan" after the saint Tipu Mastan Aulia of [Arcot](/wiki/Arcot,_Vellore "Arcot, Vellore"). Being illiterate, Hyder was very particular in giving his eldest son a religious education and a very early exposure to military and political affairs. At age of 17 onwards Tipu was given charge of diplomatic and military missions and supported his father Hyder in his wars.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Tipu's father, [Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali"), was a military officer in service to the [Kingdom of Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore") who had become the *de facto* ruler of Mysore in 1761 while his mother Fatima Fakhr-un-Nisa was the daughter of Mir Muin-ud-Din, the governor of the fort of [Kadapa](/wiki/Kadapa "Kadapa"). Hyder Ali appointed able teachers to give Tipu an early education in subjects like [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), Persian, Arabic, [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"), [Beary](/wiki/Beary "Beary"), [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), [Islamic jurisprudence](/wiki/Fiqh "Fiqh"), [riding](/wiki/Equestrianism "Equestrianism"), shooting and fencing.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan20056-18)[[20]](#cite_note-AnwarH-21)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWenger20174-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Language

Tipu Sultan's mother tongue was [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"). The French noted that "Their language is Moorish[Urdu] but they also speak Persian."[[23]](#cite_note-24) *Moors* at the time was a European designation for Urdu: "I have a deep knowledge [*je possède à fond*] of the common tongue of India, called *Moors* by the English, and *Ourdouzebain* by the natives of the land."[[24]](#cite_note-25)

**Religion**

Tipu hailed from a religious family and used to perform his 5 daily prayers, he was also peer o murshid of popular saint [Bande Nawaz](/wiki/Bande_Nawaz "Bande Nawaz") also known as gesu daraz, prayers and celebration of [Eid al-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_al-Fitr "Eid al-Fitr") and [Eid al-Adha](/wiki/Eid_al-Adha "Eid al-Adha") were performed in kingdom along with celebration of [Mawlid](/wiki/Mawlid "Mawlid"), [Ashura](/wiki/Ashura "Ashura") and [Urs of sufi saints](/wiki/Urs "Urs")

Tipu was also secular to allow and celebrate other religion festivals as well.

## Early military service

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/War_coat_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg/250px-War_coat_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)](/wiki/File:War_coat_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)

War coat used by Tipu Sultan of Mysore.c. 1785-1790

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Flintlock_Blunderbuss_Tipoo_Sahib_Seringapatam_1793_1794.jpg/250px-Flintlock_Blunderbuss_Tipoo_Sahib_Seringapatam_1793_1794.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flintlock_Blunderbuss_Tipoo_Sahib_Seringapatam_1793_1794.jpg)

A [flintlock](/wiki/Flintlock "Flintlock") [blunderbuss](/wiki/Blunderbuss "Blunderbuss"), built for Tipu Sultan in [Srirangapatna](/wiki/Srirangapatna "Srirangapatna"), 1793–94. Tipu Sultan used many Western craftsmen, and this gun reflects the most up-to-date technologies of the time.[[25]](#cite_note-Moma-26)

### Early Conflicts

Tipu Sultan was instructed in military tactics by French officers in the employment of his father. At age 15, he accompanied his father against the British in the [First Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Mysore_War "First Anglo-Mysore War") in 1766. He commanded a corps of cavalry in the invasion of [Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_region "Carnatic region") in 1767 at age 16. He also took part in the [First Anglo-Maratha War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Maratha_War "First Anglo-Maratha War") of 1775–1779.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-27)

[Alexander Beatson](/wiki/Alexander_Beatson "Alexander Beatson"), who published a volume on the [Fourth Mysore War](/wiki/Fourth_Mysore_War "Fourth Mysore War") entitled *View of the Origin and Conduct of the War with Tippoo Sultaun*, described Tipu Sultan as follows: "His stature was about five feet eight inches; he had a short neck, square shoulders, and was rather corpulent: his limbs were small, particularly his feet and hands; he had large full eyes, small arched eyebrows, and an aquiline nose; his complexion was fair, and the general expression of his countenance, not void of dignity".[[27]](#cite_note-Beatson1-28)

### Second Anglo-Mysore War

Main article: [Second Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Mysore_War "Second Anglo-Mysore War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Battle_of_pollilur.jpg/250px-Battle_of_pollilur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_pollilur.jpg)

[Mural](/wiki/Mural "Mural") of the [Battle of Pollilur](/wiki/Battle_of_Pollilur "Battle of Pollilur") on the walls of Tipu's summer palace, painted to celebrate his triumph over the British

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Tipu_Sultan%27s_cannon.jpg/250px-Tipu_Sultan%27s_cannon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tipu_Sultan%27s_cannon.jpg)

Very small Cannon used by Tipu Sultan's forces now in Government Museum (Egmore), Chennai

In 1779, the British captured the French-controlled port of [Mahé](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9,_India "Mahé, India") which Tipu had placed under his protection, providing some troops for its defence. In response, Hyder launched an invasion of the Carnatic, with the aim of driving the British out of [Madras](/wiki/Madras "Madras").[[28]](#cite_note-29) During this campaign in September 1780, Tipu Sultan was dispatched by Hyder Ali with 10,000 men and 18 guns to intercept [Colonel William Baillie](/wiki/William_Baillie_(East_India_Company_officer) "William Baillie (East India Company officer)") who was on his way to join Sir [Hector Munro](/wiki/Hector_Munro,_8th_of_Novar "Hector Munro, 8th of Novar"). In the [Battle of Pollilur](/wiki/Battle_of_Pollilur "Battle of Pollilur"), Tipu defeated Baillie. Out of 360 Europeans, about 200 were captured alive, and the sepoys, who were about 3800 men, suffered very high casualties. Munro was moving south with a separate force to join Baillie, but on hearing the news of the defeat he retreated to Madras, abandoning his artillery in a water tank at [Kanchipuram](/wiki/Kanchipuram "Kanchipuram").[[29]](#cite_note-30)

Tipu Sultan defeated Colonel Braithwaite at Annagudi near Tanjore on 18 February 1782. Braithwaite's forces, consisting of 100 Europeans, 300 cavalry, 1400 [sepoys](/wiki/Sepoy "Sepoy") and 10 field pieces, was the standard size of the colonial armies. Tipu Sultan seized all guns and took the detachment prisoner. In December 1781 Tipu Sultan seized Chittur from the British. Tipu Sultan had gained sufficient military experience by the time Hyder Ali died on Friday, 6 December 1782. Some historians put Hyder Ali's death at 2 or 3 days later or before due to the Hijri date being 1 [Muharram](/wiki/Muharram "Muharram"), 1197 as per some records in Persian (which can result in a difference of 1 to 3 days due to the Lunar Calendar). He became the ruler of Mysore on Sunday, 22 December 1782 (the inscriptions in some of Tipu's [regalia](/wiki/Regalia "Regalia") show it as 20 [Muharram](/wiki/Muharram "Muharram"), 1197 [Hijri](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar") Sunday) in a simple coronation ceremony.
He subsequently worked on to check the advances of the British by making alliances with the [Marathas](/wiki/Marathas "Marathas") and the [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"). The Second Mysore War came to an end with the 1784 [Treaty of Mangalore](/wiki/Treaty_of_Mangalore "Treaty of Mangalore").[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[30]](#cite_note-31)

## Ruler of Mysore

On 29 December 1782, Tipu Sultan crowned himself *Badshah* or Emperor of Mysore with the title Nawab Tipu Sultan Bahadur at age 32, and struck coinage.[[31]](#cite_note-Brittlebank_2022-32)

[Tipu Sultan's Summer Palace](/wiki/Daria_Daulat_Bagh "Daria Daulat Bagh") at [Srirangapatna](/wiki/Srirangapatna "Srirangapatna"), Karnataka

The [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") under its new [Peshwa](/wiki/Peshwa "Peshwa") [Madhavrao I](/wiki/Madhavrao_I "Madhavrao I") regained most of Indian subcontinent, twice defeating Tipu's father in 1764 and then in 1767. In 1767 Maratha Peshwa Madhavrao defeated both Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan and entered Srirangapatna, the capital of Mysore. Hyder Ali accepted the authority of Madhavrao who gave him the title of Nawab of Mysore.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201172-33)

Subsequently, to escape the treaty, Tipu tried to take some Maratha forts in Southern India captured by in the previous war and also stopped the tribute to Marathas which was promised by Hyder Ali.[[33]](#cite_note-tipu_2-34) This brought Tipu in direct conflict with the Marathas, leading to [Maratha–Mysore War](/wiki/Maratha%E2%80%93Mysore_War "Maratha–Mysore War")[[33]](#cite_note-tipu_2-34)
Conflicts between Mysore (under Tipu) and Marathas:

* [Siege of Nargund](/wiki/Second_siege_of_Nargund "Second siege of Nargund") during February 1785 won by Mysore
* [Siege of Badami](/wiki/Siege_of_Badami "Siege of Badami") during May 1786 in which Mysore surrendered
* [Siege of Adoni](/wiki/Siege_of_Adoni "Siege of Adoni") during June 1786 won by Mysore
* [Battle of Gajendragad](/wiki/Battle_of_Gajendragad "Battle of Gajendragad"), June 1786 won by Marathas
* [Battle of Savanur](/wiki/Battle_of_Savanur "Battle of Savanur") during October 1786 won by Mysore
* [Siege of Bahadur Benda](/wiki/Siege_of_Bahadur_Benda "Siege of Bahadur Benda") during January 1787 won by Mysore

Conflict ended with [Treaty of Gajendragad](/wiki/Gajendragarh#Treaty_of_Gajendragad "Gajendragarh") in March 1787, as per which Tipu returned all the territory captured by [Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali") to [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire").[[33]](#cite_note-tipu_2-34)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESen199554-35) Tipu would release Kalopant and return Adoni, Kittur, and Nargund to their previous rulers. Badami would be ceded to the Marathas and Tipu would also pay an annual tribute totaling 12 lakhs for an agreed period of 4 years to the Marathas. In return, Tipu Sultan would get all the region that he had captured during the war. This included Gajendragarh and Dharwar.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005105-36)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESen199559-37) The Marathas in return agreed to recognize his authority and to address Tipu sultan as "Nabob Tipu Sultan Futteh Ally Khan".[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESen199559-37) However the Marathas ultimately reneged on the treaty and in the [Fourth Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Fourth_Anglo-Mysore_War "Fourth Anglo-Mysore War") the Marathas presented their support to the British East India Company which helped the British to take over Mysore in 1799.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoienuddin2000-38)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005105Sen199559-39)

### The Invasion of Malabar (1766–1790)

Main article: [Mysorean invasion of Malabar](/wiki/Mysorean_invasion_of_Malabar "Mysorean invasion of Malabar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Tippoo_Sahib_at_the_lines_of_Travancore_in_the_1850s.jpg/250px-Tippoo_Sahib_at_the_lines_of_Travancore_in_the_1850s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tippoo_Sahib_at_the_lines_of_Travancore_in_the_1850s.jpg)

Tipu Sultan at the lines of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore").

In 1766 when he was 15 years old Tipu accompanied his father on an invasion of Malabar. After the incident- [Siege of Tellicherry](/wiki/Siege_of_Tellicherry "Siege of Tellicherry") in [Thalassery](/wiki/Thalassery "Thalassery") in North [Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_District "Malabar District"),[[39]](#cite_note-40) Hyder Ali started losing his territories in Malabar. Tipu came from [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore") to reinstate the authority over Malabar. After the [Battle of the Nedumkotta (1789–90](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Nedumkotta "Battle of the Nedumkotta")), due to the monsoon flood, the stiff resistance of the Travancore forces and news about the attack of British in [Srirangapatnam](/wiki/Srirangapatnam "Srirangapatnam") he went back.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

### Third Anglo-Mysore War

Main article: [Third Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Mysore_War "Third Anglo-Mysore War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Tippu%27s_cannon.jpg/250px-Tippu%27s_cannon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tippu%27s_cannon.jpg)

Cannon used by Tipu Sultan's forces at the battle of [Srirangapatna](/wiki/Srirangapatna "Srirangapatna") 1799

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Surrender_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg/250px-Surrender_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surrender_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)

General [Lord Cornwallis](/wiki/Charles_Cornwallis,_1st_Marquess_Cornwallis "Charles Cornwallis, 1st Marquess Cornwallis"), receiving two of Tipu Sultan's sons as hostages in the year 1793.

In 1789, Tipu Sultan disputed the acquisition by [Dharma Raja of Travancore](/wiki/Dharma_Raja_of_Travancore "Dharma Raja of Travancore") of two [Dutch-held](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") fortresses in [Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin"). In December 1789 he massed troops at [Coimbatore](/wiki/Coimbatore "Coimbatore"), and on 28 December [made an attack](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Nedumkotta "Battle of the Nedumkotta") on the lines of Travancore, knowing that Travancore was (according to the [Treaty of Mangalore](/wiki/Treaty_of_Mangalore "Treaty of Mangalore")) an ally of the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWenger201711–-42) On account of the staunch resistance by the Travancore army, Tipu was unable to break through the Tranvancore lines and the Maharajah of Travancore appealed to the East India Company for help. In response, [Lord Cornwallis](/wiki/Charles_Cornwallis,_1st_Marquess_Cornwallis "Charles Cornwallis, 1st Marquess Cornwallis") mobilised company and British military forces, and formed alliances with the Marathas and the Nizam of Hyderabad to oppose Tipu. In 1790 the company forces advanced, taking control of much of the Coimbatore district.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWenger201711–-42) Tipu counter-attacked, regaining much of the territory, although the British continued to hold Coimbatore itself. He then descended into the Carnatic, eventually reaching [Pondicherry](/wiki/Pondicherry_(city) "Pondicherry (city)"), where he attempted without success to draw the French into the conflict.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWenger201711–-42)

In 1791 his opponents advanced on all fronts, with the main British force under Cornwallis [taking Bangalore](/wiki/Siege_of_Bangalore "Siege of Bangalore") and threatening Srirangapatna. Tipu harassed the British supply and communication and embarked on a "scorched earth" policy of denying local resources to the British.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWenger201711–-42) In this last effort he was successful, as the lack of provisions forced Cornwallis to withdraw to Bangalore rather than attempt a siege of Srirangapatna. Following the withdrawal, Tipu sent forces to Coimbatore, which they retook [after a lengthy siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Coimbatore "Siege of Coimbatore").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWenger201711–-42)

The 1792 campaign was a failure for Tipu. The allied army was well-supplied, and Tipu was unable to prevent the junction of forces from Bangalore and Bombay before Srirangapatna.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWenger201711–-42) After [about two weeks of siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Seringapatam_(1792) "Siege of Seringapatam (1792)"), Tipu opened negotiations for terms of surrender. In the [ensuing treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Seringapatam "Treaty of Seringapatam"), he was forced to cede half his territories to the allies,[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-27) and deliver two of his sons as hostages until he paid in full three crores and thirty lakhs rupees fixed as war indemnity to the British for the campaign against him. He paid the amount in two instalments and got back his sons from Madras.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWenger201711–-42)

### Napoleon's attempt at a junction

Main article: [Franco-Indian alliances](/wiki/Franco-Indian_alliances "Franco-Indian alliances")

In 1794, with the support of French Republican officers, Tipu allegedly helped found the [Jacobin Club of Mysore](/wiki/Jacobin_Club_of_Mysore "Jacobin Club of Mysore") for 'framing laws comfortable with the laws of the Republic'. He planted a Liberty Tree and declared himself Citizen Tipoo.[[42]](#cite_note-43) In a 2005 paper, historian Jean Boutier argued that the club's existence, and Tipu's involvement in it, was fabricated by the East India Company in order to justify British military intervention against Tipu.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

One of the motivations of Napoleon's [invasion of Egypt](/wiki/French_campaign_in_Egypt_and_Syria "French campaign in Egypt and Syria") was to establish a junction with India against the British. Bonaparte wished to establish a French presence in the Middle East, with the ultimate dream of linking with Tippoo Sahib.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Napoleon assured the [French Directory](/wiki/French_Directory "French Directory") that "as soon as he had conquered Egypt, he will establish relations with the Indian princes and, together with them, attack the English in their possessions."[[45]](#cite_note-books.google.com-46) According to a 13 February 1798 report by [Talleyrand](/wiki/Talleyrand "Talleyrand"): "Having occupied and fortified Egypt, we shall send a force of 15,000 men from [Suez](/wiki/Suez "Suez") to India, to join the forces of Tipu-Sahib and drive away the English."[[45]](#cite_note-books.google.com-46) Napoleon was unsuccessful in this strategy, losing the [Siege of Acre](/wiki/Siege_of_Acre_(1799) "Siege of Acre (1799)") in 1799 and at the [Battle of Abukir](/wiki/Battle_of_Abukir_(1801) "Battle of Abukir (1801)") in 1801.[[46]](#cite_note-karsh11-47)

> Although I never supposed that he (*[Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")*) possessed, allowing for some difference of education, the liberality of conduct and political views which were sometimes exhibited by old [Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali"), yet I did think he might have shown the same resolved and dogged spirit of resolution which induced Tipu Sahib to die manfully upon the breach of his capital city with his sabre clenched in his hand.

— Sir [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott"), commenting on the abdication of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") Bonaparte in 1814

## Death

Further information: [Fourth Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Fourth_Anglo-Mysore_War "Fourth Anglo-Mysore War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Tipu_Sultan%2C_Indian_warrior_Emperor_of_Mysore.gif/250px-Tipu_Sultan%2C_Indian_warrior_Emperor_of_Mysore.gif)](/wiki/File:Tipu_Sultan,_Indian_warrior_Emperor_of_Mysore.gif)

Tipu Sultan confronts his opponents during the [Siege of Srirangapatna.](/wiki/Siege_of_Seringapatam_(1799) "Siege of Seringapatam (1799)")

[Horatio Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson "Horatio Nelson") defeated [François-Paul Brueys D'Aigalliers](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Paul_Brueys_D%27Aigalliers "François-Paul Brueys D'Aigalliers") at the [Battle of the Nile](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Nile "Battle of the Nile") in Egypt in 1798. Three armies marched into Mysore in 1799—one from [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") and two British, one of which included Arthur Wellesley.[[47]](#cite_note-48) They besieged the capital [Srirangapatna](/wiki/Srirangapatna "Srirangapatna") in the [Fourth Mysore War](/wiki/Fourth_Mysore_War "Fourth Mysore War").[[48]](#cite_note-49) There were more than 60,000 soldiers of the British East India Company, approximately 4,000 Europeans and the rest Indians; while Tipu Sultan's forces numbered only around 30,000. The betrayal by Tipu Sultan's ministers in working with the British and weakening the walls to make an easy path for the British.[[49]](#cite_note-hindu-50)[[50]](#cite_note-Sunderlal_2018_p._364-51) The death of Tipu Sultan led British General Harris to exclaim "Now India is ours."[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoienuddin2000-38)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

When the British [broke through](/wiki/Battle_of_Srirangapatna "Battle of Srirangapatna") the city walls, French military advisers told Tipu Sultan[[51]](#cite_note-Tipu's_french_advisors-52) to escape via secret passages and to fight the rest of the wars from other forts, but he refused.[[52]](#cite_note-53) Tipu famously said "Better to live one day as a tiger than a thousand years as a sheep".[[53]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Tipu_death.jpg/250px-Tipu_death.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tipu_death.jpg)

*[The Last Effort and Fall of Tippoo Sultaun](/wiki/The_Last_Effort_and_Fall_of_Tippoo_Sultaun "The Last Effort and Fall of Tippoo Sultaun")* by [Henry Singleton](/wiki/Henry_Singleton_(painter) "Henry Singleton (painter)"), c. 1800

Tipu Sultan was killed at the Hoally (Diddy) Gateway, which was located 300 yards (270 m) from the N.E. Angle of the Srirangapatna Fort.[[54]](#cite_note-55) He was buried the next afternoon at the [Gumaz](/wiki/Gumbaz,_Seringapatam "Gumbaz, Seringapatam"), next to the grave of his father. Many members of the British East India Company believed that [Nawab of Carnatic](/wiki/Nawab_of_Carnatic "Nawab of Carnatic") [Umdat Ul-Umra](/wiki/Umdat_Ul-Umra "Umdat Ul-Umra") secretly provided assistance to Tipu Sultan during the war and sought his deposition after 1799.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These five men include Mir Sadiq, Purnaiya, two military commanders Saiyed Saheb and Qamaruddin, and Mir Nadim, commandant of the fort of Seringapatam. The episode of treachery as narrated by Hasan starts with the disobedience of Tipu's instructions.[[55]](#cite_note-56) When he died there were jubilant celebrations in Britain, with authors, playwrights and painters creating works to celebrate it.[[56]](#cite_note-57) The death of Tipu Sultan was celebrated with declaration of public holiday in Britain.[[57]](#cite_note-te_Deum-58)

## Administration

Tipu introduced a new calendar, new coinage, and seven new government departments, during his reign, and made military innovations in the use of rocketry.

### Mysorean rockets

Main article: [Mysorean rockets](/wiki/Mysorean_rockets "Mysorean rockets")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Indian_soldier_of_Tipu_Sultan%27s_army.jpg/250px-Indian_soldier_of_Tipu_Sultan%27s_army.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indian_soldier_of_Tipu_Sultan%27s_army.jpg)

A soldier from Tipu Sultan's army, using his [rocket](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket") as a flagstaff.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Congreve_rockets.gif/250px-Congreve_rockets.gif)](/wiki/File:Congreve_rockets.gif)

Tipu Sultan organised his [Rocket](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket") artillery brigades known as *Cushoons*, Tipu Sultan expanded the number of servicemen in the various *Cushoons* from 1500 to almost 5000. The [Mysorean rockets](/wiki/Mysorean_rockets "Mysorean rockets") utilised by Tipu Sultan, were later updated by the British and successively employed during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").

Dr [APJ Abdul Kalam](/wiki/APJ_Abdul_Kalam "APJ Abdul Kalam"), the former [President of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India"), in his Tipu Sultan Shaheed Memorial Lecture in Bangalore (30 November 1991), called Tipu Sultan the innovator of the world's first war rocket. Two of these rockets, captured by the British at Srirangapatna, were displayed in the [Royal Artillery Museum](/wiki/Firepower_%E2%80%93_The_Royal_Artillery_Museum "Firepower – The Royal Artillery Museum") in London. According to historian Dr [Dulari Qureshi](/wiki/Dulari_Qureshi "Dulari Qureshi") Tipu Sultan was a fierce warrior king and was so quick in his movement that it seemed to the enemy that he was fighting on many fronts at the same time.[[49]](#cite_note-hindu-50) Tipu managed to subdue all the petty kingdoms in the south. He was also one of the few Indian rulers to have defeated British armies.

Tipu Sultan's father had expanded on [Mysore's use of rocketry](/wiki/Mysorean_rockets "Mysorean rockets"), making critical innovations in the rockets themselves and the military logistics of their use. He deployed as many as 1,200 specialised troops in his army to operate rocket launchers. These men were skilled in operating the weapons and were trained to launch their rockets at an angle calculated from the diameter of the cylinder and the distance to the target. The rockets had twin side sharpened blades mounted on them, and when fired *en masse*, spun and wreaked significant damage against a large army. Tipu greatly expanded the use of rockets after Hyder's death, deploying as many as 5,000 rocketeers at a time.[[58]](#cite_note-5000_rockets-59) The rockets deployed by Tipu during the [Battle of Pollilur](/wiki/Battle_of_Pollilur_(1780) "Battle of Pollilur (1780)") were much more advanced than those the British East India Company had previously seen, chiefly because of the use of iron tubes for holding the propellant; this enabled higher thrust and longer range for the missiles (up to 2 km range).[[58]](#cite_note-5000_rockets-59)[[11]](#cite_note-Narasimha-12)

British accounts describe the use of the rockets during the third and fourth wars.[[59]](#cite_note-60) During the climactic battle at Srirangapatna in 1799, British shells struck a magazine containing rockets, causing it to explode and send a towering cloud of black smoke with cascades of exploding white light rising up from the battlements. After Tipu's defeat in the fourth war the British captured a number of the Mysorean rockets. These became influential in British rocket development, inspiring the [Congreve rocket](/wiki/Congreve_rocket "Congreve rocket"), which was soon put into use in the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").[[11]](#cite_note-Narasimha-12)

### Navy

In 1786 Tipu Sultan, again following the lead of his father, decided to build a navy consisting of 20 battleships of 72 cannons and 20 frigates of 65 cannons. In the year 1790 he appointed Kamaluddin as his *Mir Bahar* and established massive dockyards at [Jamalabad](/wiki/Jamalabad "Jamalabad") and Majidabad. Tipu Sultan's board of admiralty consisted of 11 commanders in service of a *Mir Yam*. A *Mir Yam* led 30 admirals and each one of them had two ships. Tipu Sultan ordered that the ships have [copper-bottoms](/wiki/Copper_sheathing "Copper sheathing"), an idea that increased the longevity of the ships and was introduced to Tipu by [Admiral Suffren](/wiki/Pierre_Andr%C3%A9_de_Suffren_de_Saint_Tropez "Pierre André de Suffren de Saint Tropez").[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201122-61)

### Army

Due to their perpetual battle engagements, Haidar and Tipu required a disciplined standing army. Thus, [Rajputs](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput"), Muslims and able tribal men were enrolled for full time service replacing the local militia called the *Kandachar*[[61]](#cite_note-62) force of agricultural origin which existed in the Mysore army earlier. The removal of the [Vokkaligas](/wiki/Vokkaliga "Vokkaliga") from the local militia which had taken part in wars for centuries and the imposition of higher taxes on them in place of their quit rent led indirectly to the implementation of [Ryotwari](/wiki/Ryotwari "Ryotwari") system. Now the [Ryots](/wiki/Ryot#Ryotwari_system "Ryot") could not rely upon slaves for their agricultural activities since their slaves were enrolled in the army in some places. Besides paying higher taxes they had to endure the additional responsibility of feeding the slaves and financing their marriages. This led to the weakening of the system of slavery in [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore").[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### Economy

Main article: [Economy of the Kingdom of Mysore](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Kingdom_of_Mysore "Economy of the Kingdom of Mysore")

Further information: [Mysore silk](/wiki/Mysore_silk "Mysore silk") and [Economic history of India](/wiki/Economic_history_of_India "Economic history of India")

The peak of [Mysore's economic power](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Kingdom_of_Mysore "Economy of the Kingdom of Mysore") was under Tipu Sultan in the late 18th century. Along with his father Hyder Ali, he embarked on an ambitious program of economic development, aiming to increase the wealth and revenue of Mysore.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParthasarathi2011207-64) Under his reign, Mysore overtook [Bengal Subah](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah") as [India's dominant economic power](/wiki/Economic_history_of_India "Economic history of India"), with highly productive [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") and [textile manufacturing](/wiki/Textile_manufacturing "Textile manufacturing").[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParthasarathi201138,_271-65) Mysore's average income was five times higher than [subsistence](/wiki/Subsistence "Subsistence") level at the time.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParthasarathi201145-66)

Tipu Sultan laid the foundation for the construction of the [Kannambadi](/wiki/Kannambadi "Kannambadi") dam (present-day [Krishna Raja Sagara](/wiki/Krishna_Raja_Sagara "Krishna Raja Sagara") or KRS dam) on the [Kaveri](/wiki/Kaveri "Kaveri") river, as attested by an extant stone plaque bearing his name, but was unable to begin the construction.[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68) The dam was later built and opened in 1938. It is a major source of drinking water for the people of Mysore and Bangalore.

The [Mysore silk](/wiki/Mysore_silk "Mysore silk") industry was first initiated during the reign of Tipu Sultan.[[68]](#cite_note-Hunter512-69) He sent an expert to [Bengal Subah](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah") to study [silk](/wiki/Silk "Silk") cultivation and processing, after which Mysore began developing polyvoltine silk.[[15]](#cite_note-Global_Silk_Industry-16)

The greater prominence of the [Channapatna toys](/wiki/Channapatna_toys "Channapatna toys") can be traced to patronage from Tipu Sultan, the historic ruler of [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore"), though these toys existed before this period historically given as gifts as part of [Dusshera](/wiki/Mysore_Dasara "Mysore Dasara") celebrations. It is known that he was an ardent admirer of arts, and in particular of woodwork.[[16]](#cite_note-Handmade-17)

#### Road development

Tipu Sultan was considered as pioneer of road construction, especially in Malabar, as part of his campaigns, he connected most of the cities by roads.[[69]](#cite_note-Edgar185-70)

### Foreign relations

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Louis_XVI_Receives_the_Ambassadors_of_Tipu_Sultan_1788_Voyer_after_Emile_Wattier_19th_century.jpg/250px-Louis_XVI_Receives_the_Ambassadors_of_Tipu_Sultan_1788_Voyer_after_Emile_Wattier_19th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis_XVI_Receives_the_Ambassadors_of_Tipu_Sultan_1788_Voyer_after_Emile_Wattier_19th_century.jpg)

[Louis XVI](/wiki/Louis_XVI "Louis XVI") receives the ambassadors of Tipu Sultan in 1788. Tipu Sultan is known to have sent many diplomatic missions to France, the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [Sultanate of Oman](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Oman "Sultanate of Oman"), [Zand dynasty](/wiki/Zand_dynasty "Zand dynasty") and [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire").[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Mughal Empire

Both Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan owed nominal allegiance to the [Mughal Emperor](/wiki/Mughal_Emperor "Mughal Emperor") [Shah Alam II](/wiki/Shah_Alam_II "Shah Alam II"); both were described as [Nabobs](/wiki/Nabob "Nabob") by the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") in all existing treaties. But unlike the [Nawab of Carnatic](/wiki/Nawab_of_Carnatic "Nawab of Carnatic"), they did not acknowledge the overlordship of the [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrittlebank1999[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_September_2023]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (September_2023)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-72)

Immediately after his coronation as *Badshah*, Tipu Sultan sought the investiture of the Mughal emperor. He earned the title *"Nasib-ud-Daula"* with the heavy heart of those loyal to Shah Alam II. Tipu was a selfdeclared "[Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan")" this fact drew towards him the hostility of [Nizam Ali Khan](/wiki/Nizam_Ali_Khan "Nizam Ali Khan"), the Nizam of Hyderabad, who clearly expressed his hostility by dissuading the Mughal emperor and laying claims on Mysore. Disheartened, Tipu Sultan began to establish contacts with other Muslim rulers of that period.[[72]](#cite_note-auto2-73)

Tipu Sultan was the master of his own diplomacy with foreign nations, in his quest to rid India of the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") and to ensure the international strength of [France](/wiki/France "France"). Like his father before him he fought battles on behalf of foreign nations which were not in the best interests of Shah Alam II.

After [Ghulam Qadir](/wiki/Ghulam_Kadir "Ghulam Kadir") had Shah Alam II blinded on 10 August 1788, Tipu Sultan is believed to have broken into tears.[[73]](#cite_note-74)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/The_storming_of_Seringapatam_-_John_Vendramini%2C_1802_-_BL_P779.jpg/250px-The_storming_of_Seringapatam_-_John_Vendramini%2C_1802_-_BL_P779.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_storming_of_Seringapatam_-_John_Vendramini,_1802_-_BL_P779.jpg)

Tipu Sultan's forces during the [Siege of Srirangapatna](/wiki/Siege_of_Seringapatam_(1799) "Siege of Seringapatam (1799)").

After the [Fall of Seringapatam](/wiki/Siege_of_Seringapatam_(1799) "Siege of Seringapatam (1799)") in 1799, the blind emperor did remorse for Tipu, but maintained his confidence in the [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad"), who had now made peace with the British.

Afghanistan

After facing substantial threats from the [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"), Tipu Sultan began to correspond with [Zaman Shah Durrani](/wiki/Zaman_Shah_Durrani "Zaman Shah Durrani"), the ruler of the Afghan [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire"), so they could defeat the British and Marathas. Initially, Zaman Shah agreed to help Tipu, but the Persian attack on Afghanistan's Western border diverted its forces, and hence no help could be provided to Tipu.

Ottoman Empire

In 1787, Tipu Sultan sent an embassy to the [Ottoman](/wiki/Turkish_Ottoman_Empire "Turkish Ottoman Empire") capital Constantinople, to the Ottoman [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan") [Abdul Hamid I](/wiki/Abdul_Hamid_I "Abdul Hamid I") requesting urgent assistance against the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company"). Tipu Sultan requested the Ottoman Sultan to send him troops and military experts. Furthermore, Tipu Sultan also requested permission from the Ottomans to contribute to the maintenance of the [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") shrines in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"), [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina"), [Najaf](/wiki/Najaf "Najaf") and [Karbala](/wiki/Karbala "Karbala").

However, the Ottomans were themselves in crisis and still recuperating from the devastating [Austro-Ottoman War](/wiki/Austro-Turkish_War_of_1716%E2%80%9318 "Austro-Turkish War of 1716–18") and a new conflict with the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") had begun, for which Ottoman Turkey needed British alliance to keep off the Russians, hence it could not risk being hostile to the British in the Indian theatre.

Due to the Ottoman inability to organise a fleet in the Indian Ocean, Tipu Sultan's ambassadors returned home only with gifts from their Ottoman brothers.

Nevertheless, Tipu Sultan's correspondence with the Ottoman Empire and particularly its new Sultan [Selim III](/wiki/Selim_III "Selim III") continued till his final battle in the year 1799.[[72]](#cite_note-auto2-73)

Persia and Oman

Like his father before him, Tipu Sultan maintained friendly relations with [Mohammad Ali Khan](/wiki/Mohammad_Ali_Khan_Zand "Mohammad Ali Khan Zand"), ruler of the [Zand dynasty](/wiki/Zand_dynasty "Zand dynasty") in [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia"). Tipu Sultan also maintained correspondence with [Hamad bin Said](/wiki/Hamad_bin_Said "Hamad bin Said"), the ruler of the [Sultanate of Oman](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Oman "Sultanate of Oman").[[74]](#cite_note-75)

Qing China

Tipu's and Mysore's tryst with silk began in the early 1780s when he received an ambassador from the [Qing dynasty-ruled China](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") at his court. The ambassador presented him with a silk cloth. Tipu was said to be enchanted by the item to such an extent that he resolved to introduce its production in his kingdom. He sent a return journey to China, which returned after twelve years.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

France

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Louis-Fran%C3%A7ois_Baron_Lejeune_001.jpg/500px-Louis-Fran%C3%A7ois_Baron_Lejeune_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis-Fran%C3%A7ois_Baron_Lejeune_001.jpg)

In his attempts to junction with Tipu Sultan, [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") annexed [Ottoman Egypt](/wiki/Ottoman_Egypt "Ottoman Egypt") in the year 1798.

Both Hyder Ali and Tipu sought an alliance with the French, the only European power still strong enough to challenge the British East India Company in the subcontinent. In 1782, Louis XVI concluded an alliance with the [Peshwa](/wiki/Peshwa "Peshwa") [Madhu Rao Narayan](/wiki/Madhavrao_II "Madhavrao II"). This treaty enabled [Bussy](/wiki/Marquis_de_Bussy-Castelnau "Marquis de Bussy-Castelnau") to move his troops to the [Isle de France](/wiki/Isle_de_France_(Mauritius) "Isle de France (Mauritius)") (now [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius")). In the same year, French Admiral [De Suffren](/wiki/Pierre_Andr%C3%A9_de_Suffren_de_Saint_Tropez "Pierre André de Suffren de Saint Tropez") ceremonially presented a portrait of Louis XVI to Haidar Ali and sought his alliance.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

[Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") conquered [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") in an attempt to link with Tipu Sultan.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In February 1798, Napoleon wrote a letter to Tipu Sultan appreciating his efforts of resisting the British annexation and plans, but this letter never reached Tipu and was seized by a British spy in Muscat. The idea of a possible Tipu-Napoleon alliance alarmed the British Governor, General Sir [Richard Wellesley](/wiki/Richard_Wellesley,_1st_Marquess_Wellesley "Richard Wellesley, 1st Marquess Wellesley") (also known as Lord Wellesley), so much that he immediately started large scale preparations for a final battle against Tipu Sultan.

### Social system

#### Judicial system

Tipu Sultan appointed judges from both communities for Hindu and Muslim subjects. **Qadi** for Muslims and **Pandit** for Hindus in each province. Upper courts also had similar systems.[[77]](#cite_note-SocialScientist110-78)

#### Moral Administration

Usage of liquor and prostitution were strictly prohibited in his administration.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESastri1943269-79) Usage and agriculture of [psychedelics](/wiki/Psychedelic "Psychedelic"), such as [Cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis "Cannabis"), was also prohibited.[[79]](#cite_note-BSN211-80)

Polyandry in Kerala was prohibited by Tipu Sultan. He passed a decree for all women to cover their breasts, which was not practised in Kerala in the previous era.[[80]](#cite_note-REMiller-81)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESastri1943270-82)

### Religious policy

On a personal level, Tipu was a devout Muslim, saying his prayers daily and paying special attention to mosques in the area.[[82]](#cite_note-83) Regular endowments were made during this period to about 156 Hindu temples,[[83]](#cite_note-chetty2-84) including the famed [Ranganathaswami Temple](/wiki/Ranganathaswamy_Temple,_Srirangapatna "Ranganathaswamy Temple, Srirangapatna") at [Srirangapatna](/wiki/Srirangapatna "Srirangapatna").[[84]](#cite_note-pande-85) Many sources mention the appointment of Hindu officers in Tipu's administration[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005357–358-86) and his land grants and endowments to Hindu temples,[[86]](#cite_note-padiga3-87)[[87]](#cite_note-A._Subbaraya_Chetty_2002-88)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005360-89) which are cited as evidence for his religious tolerance.

His religious legacy has become a source of considerable controversy in India, with some groups (including Christians[[89]](#cite_note-90) and even Muslims) proclaiming him a great warrior for the faith or *Ghazi*[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrittlebank19991–3-91)[[91]](#cite_note-rpersecutor1-92) for both religious and political reasons.[[84]](#cite_note-pande-85) Various sources describe the massacres,[[92]](#cite_note-93) imprisonment[[93]](#cite_note-acc-94) and forced conversion[[94]](#cite_note-cariappa-95) of Hindus ([Kodavas of Coorg](/wiki/Captivity_of_Kodavas_at_Seringapatam "Captivity of Kodavas at Seringapatam"), [Nairs of Malabar](/wiki/Captivity_of_Nairs_at_Seringapatam "Captivity of Nairs at Seringapatam")) and Christians ([Catholics of Mangalore](/wiki/Captivity_of_Mangalorean_Catholics_at_Seringapatam "Captivity of Mangalorean Catholics at Seringapatam")), the destruction of churches[[95]](#cite_note-Lobo2-96) and temples, and the clamping down on Muslims ([Mappila of Kerala](/wiki/Malabar_Muslims "Malabar Muslims"), the [Mahdavia](/wiki/Mahdavia "Mahdavia") Muslims, the [rulers of Savanur](/wiki/Savanur_State "Savanur State") and the people of [Hyderabad State](/wiki/Hyderabad_State "Hyderabad State")), which are sometimes cited as evidence for his intolerance.

#### British accounts

Historians such as Brittlebank, Hasan, Chetty, Habib, and Saletare, amongst others, argue that controversial stories of Tipu Sultan's religious persecution of Hindus and Christians are largely derived from the work of early British authors (who were very much against Tipu Sultan's independence and harboured prejudice against the Sultan) such as [James Kirkpatrick](/wiki/James_Achilles_Kirkpatrick "James Achilles Kirkpatrick")[[96]](#cite_note-97) and [Mark Wilks](/wiki/Mark_Wilks "Mark Wilks"),[[97]](#cite_note-98) whom they do not consider to be entirely reliable and likely fabricated.[[98]](#cite_note-habib-99) A. S. Chetty argues that Wilks' account in particular cannot be trusted.[[99]](#cite_note-chetty111-100)

[Irfan Habib](/wiki/Irfan_Habib "Irfan Habib") and Mohibbul Hasan argue that these early British authors had a strong vested interest in presenting Tipu Sultan as a tyrant from whom the British had liberated Mysore.[[98]](#cite_note-habib-99)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005368-101) This assessment is echoed by Brittlebank in her recent work where she writes that Wilks and Kirkpatrick must be used with particular care as both authors had taken part in the wars against Tipu Sultan and were closely connected to the administrations of [Lord Cornwallis](/wiki/Charles_Cornwallis,_1st_Marquess_Cornwallis "Charles Cornwallis, 1st Marquess Cornwallis") and [Richard Wellesley, 1st Marquess Wellesley](/wiki/Richard_Wellesley,_1st_Marquess_Wellesley "Richard Wellesley, 1st Marquess Wellesley").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrittlebank19992–12-102)

#### Relations with Hindus

Tipu Sultan's treasurer was Krishna Rao, [Shamaiya Iyengar](/wiki/Shamaiya_Iyengar "Shamaiya Iyengar") was his Minister of Post and Police, his brother Ranga Iyengar was also an officer, and [Purnaiya](/wiki/Purnaiah "Purnaiah") held the very important post of "Mir Asaf". Moolchand and Sujan Rai were his chief agents at the Mughal court, and his chief "Peshkar", Suba Rao, was also a Hindu.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005357–358-86)

The Editor of Mysore Gazette reports of correspondence between his court and temples, and his having donated jewellery and deeded land grants to several temples, which he was compelled to for forming alliances with Hindu rulers. Between 1782 and 1799 Tipu Sultan issued 34 "Sanads" (deeds) of endowment to temples in his domain, while also presenting many of them with gifts of silver and gold plate.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005360-89)

The Srikanteswara Temple in Nanjangud still possesses a jeweled cup presented by the Sultan.[[87]](#cite_note-A._Subbaraya_Chetty_2002-88) He also gave a greenish [linga](/wiki/Linga "Linga"); to Ranganatha temple at Srirangapatna, he donated seven silver cups and a silver [camphor](/wiki/Camphor "Camphor") burner. This temple was hardly a stone's throw from his palace from where he would listen with equal respect to the ringing of temple bells and the [muezzin](/wiki/Muezzin "Muezzin")'s call from the mosque; to the [Lakshmikanta Temple](/wiki/Lakshmikanta_Temple,_Kalale "Lakshmikanta Temple, Kalale") at [Kalale](/wiki/Kalale "Kalale") he gifted four cups, a plate and [Spitoon](/wiki/Spitoon "Spitoon") in silver.[[86]](#cite_note-padiga3-87)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005360-89)

During the [Maratha–Mysore War](/wiki/Maratha%E2%80%93Mysore_War "Maratha–Mysore War") in 1791, a group of [Maratha](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") horsemen under Raghunath Rao Patwardhan raided the temple and *matha* of [Sringeri](/wiki/Sringeri "Sringeri") *[Shankaracharya](/wiki/Shankaracharya "Shankaracharya")*. They wounded and killed many people, including Brahmins, plundered the monastery of all its valuable possessions, and desecrated the temple by displacing the image of goddess Sarada.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005357–358-86)

The incumbent *Shankaracharya* petitioned Tipu Sultan for help. About 30 letters written in [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"), which were exchanged between Tipu Sultan's court and the [Sringeri](/wiki/Sringeri "Sringeri") [Shankaracharya](/wiki/Shankaracharya "Shankaracharya"), were discovered in 1916 by the Director of Archaeology in [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore"). Tipu Sultan expressed his indignation and grief at the news of the raid:[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005357–358-86)[[102]](#cite_note-103)

> "People who have sinned against such a holy place are sure to suffer the consequences of their misdeeds at no distant date in this Kali age in accordance with the verse: "Hasadbhih kriyate karma rudadbhir-anubhuyate" (People do [evil] deeds smilingly but suffer the consequences crying)."[[103]](#cite_note-104)

He immediately ordered the Asaf of [Bednur](/wiki/Bednur "Bednur") to supply the Swami with 200 *rahati*s (*[fanam](/wiki/Travancore_Fanam "Travancore Fanam")*s) in cash and other gifts and articles. Tipu Sultan's interest in the Sringeri temple continued for many years, and he was still writing to the Swami in the 1790s.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005359-105)

In light of this and other events, historian Irfan Habib has described Tipu Sultan as a defender of the Hindu [dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma"), who also patronised other temples including one at Melkote, for which he issued a Kannada decree that the Shrivaishnava invocatory verses there should be recited in the traditional form.[[105]](#cite_note-tipu_defender_of_Hindu_faith-106) The temple at Melkote still has gold and silver vessels with inscriptions indicating that they were presented by the Sultan. Tipu Sultan also presented four silver cups to the Lakshmikanta Temple at Kalale.[[105]](#cite_note-tipu_defender_of_Hindu_faith-106) Tipu Sultan does seem to have repossessed unauthorised grants of land made to [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") and temples, but those which had proper *sanads* (certificates) were not. It was a normal practice for any ruler, Muslim or Hindu, on his accession or on the conquest of new territory.

#### Persecution of Hindus in Melkote

Tipu killed 1200+ Iyengar Brahmins in Melkote, as part of his military campaign against the Wodeyars of Mysore and the Marathas. The Hindu Iyengar Brahmins, who were closely associated with the Sri Cheluvanarayana Swamy Temple in Melkote, were targeted during this attack.

The town of Melkote in Karnataka has stopped celebrating Deepavali as a mark of mourning for the massacre of Hindu Iyengar Brahmins carried out by Tipu Sultan's forces.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Persecution of Kodavas outside Mysore

Main article: [Captivity of Kodavas at Seringapatam](/wiki/Captivity_of_Kodavas_at_Seringapatam "Captivity of Kodavas at Seringapatam")

Tipu got Runmust Khan, the *[Nawab of Kurnool](/wiki/Kurnool "Kurnool")*, to launch a surprise attack upon the [Kodavas](/wiki/Kodava_people "Kodava people") who were besieged by the invading Muslim army. 500 were killed and over 40,000 Kodavas fled to the woods and concealed themselves in the mountains.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrabhu1999223-107) Thousands of Kodavas were seized along with the Raja and held captive at Seringapatam.[[94]](#cite_note-cariappa-95)

Mohibbul Hasan, Prof. Sheikh Ali, and other historians cast great doubt on the scale of the deportations and forced conversions in Coorg in particular. Hassan says that it is difficult to estimate the real number of [Kodava](/wiki/Kodava_people "Kodava people") captured by Tipu.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan200579-108)

In a letter to Runmust Khan, Tipu himself stated:[[108]](#cite_note-109)

> "We proceeded with the utmost speed, and, at once, made prisoners of 40,000 occasion-seeking and sedition-exciting [Kodavas](/wiki/Kodava_people "Kodava people"), who alarmed at the approach of our victorious army, had slunk into woods, and concealed themselves in lofty mountains, inaccessible even to birds. Then carrying them away from their native country (the native place of sedition) we raised them to the honour of Islam, and incorporated them into our Ahmedy corps."
> [[109]](#cite_note-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Double_paisa_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg/250px-Double_paisa_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Double_paisa_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)

Among his many innovations, Tipu introduced new coin denominations and new coin types, including this copper double paisa weighing over 23 gm. The coin on the left also contains the emblem of the [Sultanate of Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Mysore_AV_Fanam._AM_1218_%28AD_1790-1791%29._Tipu_Sultan._vz_-_MA-Shops.jpg/250px-Mysore_AV_Fanam._AM_1218_%28AD_1790-1791%29._Tipu_Sultan._vz_-_MA-Shops.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mysore_AV_Fanam._AM_1218_(AD_1790-1791)._Tipu_Sultan._vz_-_MA-Shops.jpg)

A gold coin issued by the Kingdom of Mysore during the reign of the Tipu Sultan.

The coinage of Tipu Sultan is one of the most complex and fascinating series struck in India during the 18th century. Local South India coinage had been struck in the area that became [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore") since ancient times, with the first gold coinage introduced about the 11th century (the elephant [pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda_(coin) "Pagoda (coin)")), and other pagodas continuing through the following centuries. These [pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda_(coin) "Pagoda (coin)") were always in the South Indian style until the reign of [Haidar Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali") (1761–1782), who added pagodas with Persian legends, plus a few very rare gold mohurs and silver rupees, always in the name of the [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") emperor [Shah Alam II](/wiki/Shah_Alam_II "Shah Alam II") plus the Arabic letter "ح" as the first letter of his name. His successor, Tipu Sultan, continued to issue [pagodas](/wiki/Pagoda_(coin) "Pagoda (coin)"), mohurs and [rupees](/wiki/Rupee "Rupee"), with legends that were completely new. As for copper, the new large [paisa](/wiki/Paisa "Paisa") was commenced by Haidar Ali in AH1195, two years before his death, with the elephant on the obverse, the mint on the reverse, and was continued throughout the reign of Tipu Sultan, who added other denominations. Tipu Sultan introduced a set of new [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") names for the various denominations, which appear on all of the gold and silver coins and on some of the copper. They were:

**Copper**: Qutb "قطب" for the 1/8 paisa ([Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") for the [pole star](/wiki/Pole_star "Pole star")) – Akhtar "اختر" for the 1/4 paisa (star) – Bahram "بهرام" for the 1/2 paisa (the planet [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars")) – Zohra "زهره" for the paisa (the planet [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus")) – either Othmani "عثمانی" for the double-paisa ([the third caliph](/wiki/Uthman "Uthman") of the Rashidun) or Mushtari "مشتری" (the planet [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter")).

**Silver**: Khizri "خضری" for the 1/32 rupee ([Khizr](/wiki/Khidr "Khidr") the prophet) – Kazimi "کاظمی" for the 1/16 rupee (for [Musa](/wiki/Musa_al-Kadhim "Musa al-Kadhim"), the seventh Shi'ite Imam) – Ja'fari "جعفری" for the 1/8 rupee ([Ja'far al-Sadiq](/wiki/Ja%27far_al-Sadiq "Ja'far al-Sadiq"), the sixth Shi'ite Imam) – Bâqiri "باقری" for the 1/4 rupee ([Muhammad al-Baqir](/wiki/Muhammad_al-Baqir "Muhammad al-Baqir"), the fifth Imam) – Abidi "عبیدی" for the 1/2 rupee ([Ali Zain al-'Abidin](/wiki/Ali_ibn_Husayn_Zayn_al-Abidin "Ali ibn Husayn Zayn al-Abidin"), the fourth Imam) – Imami for the rupee (reference to the [12 Shi'ite Imams](/wiki/The_Twelve_Imams "The Twelve Imams")) – Haidari "حیدری" for the double-rupee (lion, for [Ali b. Abi Talib](/wiki/Ali "Ali"), who was both the fourth caliph and the first Shi'ite Imam).

**Gold**: Faruqi "فاروقی" for the pagoda ([Umar al-Faruq](/wiki/Umar "Umar"), the second caliph) – Sadîqi "صدیقی" for the double-pagoda ([Abu Bakr](/wiki/Abu_Bakr "Abu Bakr") al-Sadiq, the first caliph) – Ahmadi "احمدی" for the four-pagoda ( "most praised ", one of the name of the [Islamic prophet](/wiki/Islamic_prophet "Islamic prophet") [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad")). During his first 4 years, the large gold coin was the [mohur](/wiki/Mohur "Mohur"), with an average weight of about 10.95g (AH1197-1200), replaced with the four-pagoda of 13.74g with the calendar change to the Mauludi "مولودی" system (AM1215-1219).

### Coinage dating system

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Coin_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg/250px-Coin_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coin_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)

2 gold Two [Pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda_(coin) "Pagoda (coin)") Coins issued by tipu Sultan

The denomination does not appear on the [Hijri](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar") dated gold coins, but was added on all the Mauludi dated pieces.

At the beginning of his first year, Tipu Sultan abandoned the [Hijri](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar") dating system and introduced the Mauludi system (from the Arabic word "walad ", which means "birth "), based on the [solar](/wiki/Solar_calendar "Solar calendar") year and the birth year of Muhammad (actually 571 AD, but for some perplexing reason reckoned as 572 by Tipu Sultan for his staff).

From the beginning of his reign, Tipu Sultan added the name of the Indian cyclic year on the large silver and gold coins, including this double-pagoda, together with his regnal year. Each of the names is Persian, though in several examples, the meaning of the names in India was different from the Iranian meaning (not indicated here). According to the Indian meanings, these are the cyclic years: Zaki "زکي" for cyclic 37, which corresponded to his year 1 ( "pure ") – Azâl "أزل" for 38 ( "[eternity](/wiki/Eternity "Eternity") ", year 2) – Jalal "جَلال" for 39 ( "splendor ", year 3) – Dalv "دَلو" for 40 (the sign of [Aquarius](/wiki/Aquarius_(constellation) "Aquarius (constellation)"), year 4) – Shâ "شاه" for 41 ( "king ", year 5) – Sârâ "سارا" for 42 ( "fragrant ", year 6) – Sarâb "سراب" for 43 ( "[mirage](/wiki/Mirage "Mirage") ", for year 7) – Shitâ "شتا" for 44 ( "winter ", year 8) – Zabarjad "زبرجد" for 45 ( "topaz ", year 9) – sahar "سَحَر" ( "[dawn](/wiki/Dawn "Dawn") ", year 10) – Sâher "ساحِر" ( "magician ", year 11).[[110]](#cite_note-111)

## Assessment and legacy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Tipu_Sultan%2C_Death_Place.jpg/250px-Tipu_Sultan%2C_Death_Place.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tipu_Sultan,_Death_Place.jpg)

The spot in [Srirangapatna](/wiki/Srirangapatna "Srirangapatna") where Tipu's body was found

**India**

The original copy of the [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") bears a painting of Tipu Sultan.[[111]](#cite_note-112)[[112]](#cite_note-113) School and college textbooks in India officially recognize him as a "freedom-fighter" along with many other rulers of the 18th century who fought European powers.[[113]](#cite_note-textbooks-114)

Assessments of Tipu Sultan have often been passionate and divided. Successive [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") governments have often celebrated Tipu Sultan's memory and monuments and relics of his rule while the [Bharatiya Janata Party](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") has been largely critical.

In 2017 the 14th Indian president [Ram Nath Kovind](/wiki/Ram_Nath_Kovind "Ram Nath Kovind") hailed Tipu Sultan in his address to the Karnataka Assembly on the occasion of the Diamond Jubilee celebrations of the state secretariat [Vidhana Soudha](/wiki/Vidhana_Soudha "Vidhana Soudha") saying "Tipu Sultan died a heroic death fighting the British. He was also a pioneer in the development and use of Mysore rockets in warfare. This technology was later adopted by the Europeans."[[114]](#cite_note-115)

Tipu Sultan has been admired by another Indian freedom fighter [Subhas Chandra Bose](/wiki/Subhas_Chandra_Bose "Subhas Chandra Bose"), the former President [Ram Nath Kovind](/wiki/Ram_Nath_Kovind "Ram Nath Kovind"),[[115]](#cite_note-116) former President [A. P. J. Abdul Kalam](/wiki/A._P._J._Abdul_Kalam "A. P. J. Abdul Kalam"), and former President [K. R. Narayanan](/wiki/K._R._Narayanan "K. R. Narayanan").[[116]](#cite_note-117)

On 21 October 1943, [Subhas Chandra Bose](/wiki/Subhas_Chandra_Bose "Subhas Chandra Bose") proclaimed the Provisional Government of [Azad Hind](/wiki/Azad_Hind "Azad Hind") (Free India), and the proclamation highlighted the "Warriors" Tipu and his father [Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali") for their names are "forever engraved in letters of gold."[[117]](#cite_note-118)

**Pakistan**

In Pakistan, Tipu Sultan is a household name and symbolises courage and bravery. There is a ship (guided missile destroyer) of [Pakistan Navy](/wiki/Pakistan_Navy "Pakistan Navy") that is named after him and is called [PNS Tippu Sultan (D-185)](/wiki/PNS_Tippu_Sultan_(D-185) "PNS Tippu Sultan (D-185)").

Tipu Sultan is also admired as a hero in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). Former [Pakistani Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Pakistan "Prime Minister of Pakistan") [Imran Khan](/wiki/Imran_Khan "Imran Khan") has said that he admires Tipu Sultan as a freedom fighter.[[118]](#cite_note-119) He has admired Tipu on multiple occasions.[[119]](#cite_note-120)

Former Prime Minister [Benazir Bhutto](/wiki/Benazir_Bhutto "Benazir Bhutto") said, "It is distressing to learn that some people believe that Tipu Sultan was anti-Hindu."[[120]](#cite_note-121) Her father, former Prime Minister [Zulfikar Ali Bhutto](/wiki/Zulfikar_Ali_Bhutto "Zulfikar Ali Bhutto"), was also an admirer of Tipu and in his library, he had kept Tipu Sultan's letters.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

In 2025, in the [Parliament of Pakistan](/wiki/Parliament_of_Pakistan "Parliament of Pakistan"), Minister [Shahid Ahmed Khattak](/wiki/Shahid_Ahmed_Khattak "Shahid Ahmed Khattak"), quoted Tipu Sultan in his criticism of the Prime Minister [Shehbaz Sharif](/wiki/Shehbaz_Sharif "Shehbaz Sharif").[[122]](#cite_note-123)

**Abroad**

In his sermon on 4 July 1800, John Russell, a [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") minister in [Providence](/wiki/Providence,_Rhode_Island "Providence, Rhode Island"), told his congregation of Tipu’s death at the hands of British soldiers: ‘here the full heart must have vent… [Tipu Sultan] defended his power with a spirit which showed he deserved it. His death was worthy of a king.’[[123]](#cite_note-124)

**Art, media and culture**

Tipu also patronised art forms such as [Ganjifa](/wiki/Ganjifa "Ganjifa") cards, effectively saving this art form.[[124]](#cite_note-125) Ganjifa card of Mysore have the GI Tag today.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

Several TV series on Tipu Sultan have been released in India and Pakistan, such as:

* *[Tipu Sultan: The Tiger Lord](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan:_The_Tiger_Lord "Tipu Sultan: The Tiger Lord")*, Pakistani television series about the sultan
* *[The Dreams of Tipu Sultan](/wiki/The_Dreams_of_Tipu_Sultan "The Dreams of Tipu Sultan")*, Indian play about the sultan by Girish Karnad
* *[The Sword of Tipu Sultan](/wiki/The_Sword_of_Tipu_Sultan "The Sword of Tipu Sultan")*, Indian television series about the sultan

### Sword and tiger

Main article: [Tipu's Tiger](/wiki/Tipu%27s_Tiger "Tipu's Tiger")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Tipu_Sultan%27s_Tiger.JPG/250px-Tipu_Sultan%27s_Tiger.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tipu_Sultan%27s_Tiger.JPG)

[Tipu Sultan's Tiger](/wiki/Tipu%27s_Tiger "Tipu's Tiger"). [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum"), London

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Inscribed_Sword_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg/250px-Inscribed_Sword_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inscribed_Sword_of_Tipu_Sultan.jpg)

Sword of Tipu Sultan. [National Museum](/wiki/National_Museum,_New_Delhi "National Museum, New Delhi"), [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi")

Tipu Sultan had lost his sword in a war with the [Nairs](/wiki/Nair "Nair") of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") during the [Battle of the Nedumkotta (1789)](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Nedumkotta "Battle of the Nedumkotta"), in which he was forced to withdraw due to the severe joint attack from the Travancore army and British army.[[126]](#cite_note-127) The [Nair army](/wiki/Nair_Brigade "Nair Brigade") under the leadership of [Raja Kesavadas](/wiki/Raja_Kesavadas "Raja Kesavadas") again defeated the army of Tipu near Aluva. The Maharaja, [Dharma Raja](/wiki/Dharma_Raja "Dharma Raja"), gave the famous sword to the Nawab of [Arcot](/wiki/Arcot "Arcot"), from whom the sword was taken as a war trophy by the British after annexing Arcot and sent to London. The sword was on display at the Wallace Collection, No. 1 Manchester Square, London.

Tipu was commonly known as the **Tiger of Mysore** and adopted this animal as the symbol (*bubri/babri*)[[127]](#cite_note-128) of his rule.[[128]](#cite_note-ModernAsianStudies-129) It is said that Tipu Sultan was hunting in the forest with a French friend. They came face to face with a tiger there. The tiger first pounced on the French soldier and killed him. Tipu's gun did not work, and his dagger fell on the ground as the tiger jumped on him. He reached for the dagger, picked it up, and killed the tiger with it. That earned him the name "the Tiger of Mysore". [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He even had French engineers build a mechanical tiger for his palace.[[129]](#cite_note-130) The device, known as [Tipu's Tiger](/wiki/Tipu%27s_Tiger "Tipu's Tiger"), is on display in the [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum"), London.[[130]](#cite_note-131) Not only did Tipu place relics of tigers around his palace and domain, but also had the emblem of a tiger on his banners and some arms and weapons. Sometimes this tiger was very ornate and had inscriptions within the drawing, alluding to Tipu's faith – Islam.[[131]](#cite_note-132) Historian [Alexander Beatson](/wiki/Alexander_Beatson "Alexander Beatson") reported that "in his palace was found a great variety of curious swords, daggers, fusils, pistols, and blunderbusses; some were of exquisite workmanship, mounted with gold, or silver, and beautifully inlaid and ornamented with tigers' heads and stripes, or with Persian and Arabic verses".[[132]](#cite_note-Beatson-133)

The last sword used by Tipu in his last [battle, at Sri Rangapatnam](/wiki/Battle_of_Srirangapatna "Battle of Srirangapatna"), and the ring worn by him were taken by the British forces as war trophies. They are kept on display at the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") London as gifts to the museum from Maj-Gen Augustus W.H. Meyrick and Nancy Dowager.[[133]](#cite_note-134) At an [auction](/wiki/Auction "Auction") in London in April 2004, [Vijay Mallya](/wiki/Vijay_Mallya "Vijay Mallya") purchased a sword of Tipu Sultan and some other historical artefacts, and brought them back to India.[[134]](#cite_note-135)

In October 2013, another sword owned by Tipu Sultan and decorated with his *babri* (tiger stripe motif) surfaced and was auctioned by [Sotheby's](/wiki/Sotheby%27s "Sotheby's").[[135]](#cite_note-136) It was purchased for £98,500[[136]](#cite_note-137) by a telephone bidder.

### Tipu Sultan Jayanti

In 2015, the [Government of Karnataka](/wiki/Government_of_Karnataka "Government of Karnataka"), under the leadership of then Chief Minister [Siddaramaiah](/wiki/Siddaramaiah "Siddaramaiah") from the [Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") party, began to celebrate Tipu's birth anniversary as the "Tipu Sultan Jayanti".[[137]](#cite_note-dc:-138) The Congress regime declared it as an annual event to be celebrated on 20 November.[[138]](#cite_note-139) It was officially celebrated in Karnataka initially by the Minority Welfare department, and later by the Kannada & Culture department. However, on 29 July 2019, the next Chief Minister [B. S. Yediyurappa](/wiki/B._S._Yediyurappa "B. S. Yediyurappa"), who belongs to the [Bharatiya Janata Party](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") (BJP), ordered the celebrations cancelled, saying: "Legislators from [Kodagu](/wiki/Kodagu_district "Kodagu district") had highlighted incidents of violence during Tipu Jayanti."

Objecting against the cancellation of the celebrations, the previous Chief Minister Siddaramaiah said: "BJP has cancelled it because of their hatred towards minorities. It's a big crime. He [Tipu] was a king of Mysore and fought against the British [as] a freedom fighter. It was during his time when the foundation was laid for the [Krishna Raja Sagara](/wiki/Krishna_Raja_Sagara "Krishna Raja Sagara") dam. He also tried to improve industry, agriculture and trade". The previous year, not a single JD(S) leader, including the then chief minister HD Kumaraswamy, attended the event, turning it into a fiasco.[[137]](#cite_note-dc:-138)

The [Lok Sabha](/wiki/Lok_Sabha "Lok Sabha") Congress leader, [Mallikarjun Kharge](/wiki/Mallikarjun_Kharge "Mallikarjun Kharge"), also earlier criticized BJP and [RSS](/wiki/Rashtriya_Swayamsevak_Sangh "Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh") for their opposition against holding the celebrations, and asked: "When RSS can celebrate [Nathuram Godse](/wiki/Nathuram_Godse "Nathuram Godse"), can't we celebrate Tipu Sultan?"[[139]](#cite_note-140)

## Family

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Gumbaz.jpg/250px-Gumbaz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gumbaz.jpg)

The mausoleum housing Tipu Sultan's tomb is another example of [Islamic architecture](/wiki/Islamic_architecture "Islamic architecture").Tipu Sultan's flag is in the foreground.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Tipu_tomb.jpg/250px-Tipu_tomb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tipu_tomb.jpg)

The tomb of Tipu Sultan at Srirangapatna. Tipu's tomb is adjacent to his mother's and father's graves.

Tipu had several wives.[[145]](#cite_note-146) His first wife was Sultan Begum Sahib also known as Padishah Begum.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005372-147) She was the daughter of Imam Sahib Bakhshi Naita from Arcot,[[147]](#cite_note-d118-148) and sister of Ghulam Husain Khan, known as the Pondicherry Nawab, a descendant of Chanda Sahib.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005372-147) They married in 1774.[[148]](#cite_note-c791-149) Another wife married at the same time was Ruqaya Banu Begum. She was the daughter of Lala Miyan Shaheed Charkoli,[[149]](#cite_note-u647-150) and the sister of Sheikh Burhanuddin.[[150]](#cite_note-i329-151) She died in February 1792 at the time of the siege of Seringapatam.[[147]](#cite_note-d118-148) Another wife was Khadija Zaman Begum. She was the daughter of Mir Sayyid Moinuddin Khan[[150]](#cite_note-i329-151) also known as Sayyid Sahib.[[151]](#cite_note-u252-152) They married in 1796. She died in childbirth in 1797.[[148]](#cite_note-c791-149) Another wife was Buranti Begum. She was the daughter of Mir Muhammad Pasand Beg, a nobleman from Delhi and her mother's father was Sayyid Muhammad Khan, once a *subedar* of Kashmir. Another wife was Roshani Begum. She was the mother of his eldest son Fath Haider.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005372-147)

His sons were [Hyder Ali Khan Sultan](/wiki/Shezada_Hyder_Ali "Shezada Hyder Ali"),[[152]](#cite_note-153)
Muin-ud-din Sultan, Abdul Khaliq Sultan, Muiz-ud-din Sultan, Muhammad Subhan Sultan, Shukrullah Sultan, Ghulam Ahmad Sultan, [Ghulam Muhammad Sultan](/wiki/Ghulam_Muhammad_Sultan_Sahib "Ghulam Muhammad Sultan Sahib"), Sarwar-ud-din Sultan, Muhammad Yasin Sultan, Jamal-ud-din Sultan and Munir-ud-din Sultan. One of his daughters was married to Husain Ali Khan.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasan2005372-147)

62. **[^](#cite_ref-63)** R. Gopal, ed. (2010). [*Tipu Sultan: The Tiger of Mysore*](https://books.google.com/books?id=tDT0UsWn7mUC&q=vokkaliga+kandachar). Mysore: Directorate of Archaeology and Museums, Government of Karnataka. p. 279. [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [813417527](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/813417527). "Since Haidar and Tipu were perpetually engaged in battles, they formed a disciplined standing army . Thus, instead of the local militia called the Kandachar force of agricultural origin which existed in the Mysore army earlier, Haidar and Tipu enrolled to their army forces the able tribal men, Muslims and Rajputs on full time service. In this way, Haidar and Tipu removed the Vokkaligas of the agricultural base from the local militia which took part in wars for centuries and in place of their quit rent, they imposed higher taxes and thus became indirectly responsible for implementation of Ryotwari system. The Ryots were not liberated from the shackles of Kandachar service; the slaves who were with them were enrolled in the army in some places. As a result, the Ryots removed from the military service could not even rely upon slaves for their agricultural activities. Hence these ryots had to endure the greater responsibility of feeding the slaves and of financing their marriages besides paying the higher taxes. So in the plains of Mysore the system of slavery was loosened."
